title: China GP fallout: paddock turns attention to strategy and penalties
meta: Published: 26 March 2026 | By Bernard “Bernie” Fletcher, Veteran Correspondent | F1CC News
caption: Paddock discussions.
body:
It’s 2026 and we’re still debating whether a driver with six penalty points after two races belongs on the grid at all.

The Chinese Grand Prix was won by Ferrari. Again. But the aftermath has been less about the scarlet victory parade and more about the aggressive strategies that defined the afternoon and the penalty accumulation that is quietly becoming the most damaging subplot of the young season. Ferrari executed a flawless offset strategy to convert Väisänen’s P8 start into a dominant victory, a 15.9‑second margin that made the rest look pedestrian. While they celebrated, the rest of the paddock was left analysing what they lost — and how to stop it happening again in Suzuka. I’ve seen this before. A team wins in China and everyone talks about catching them by Japan. They rarely do.

The stewards were busy throughout the Shanghai weekend, and one name appeared on their docket with alarming regularity. Afonso Cachinho, Ferrari’s second driver, received penalties in both the sprint and the main grand prix. He now has six penalty points on his licence and a formal warning from the governing body — in two race weekends. Six points. That’s a rate of accumulation that leads, if continued, to a race ban before the summer break. Team principal Randy Belacik, a man not known for public softness, had no choice but to address the situation in the open. The question is no longer whether Cachinho has the pace; it’s whether he can manage high‑pressure racecraft without costing himself points and positions. Maranello built a championship lead on one side of the garage. On the other, they’re watching a driver collect penalties like frequent‑flyer miles.

Meanwhile, the strategic battlefield highlighted the sheer complexity of mastering the Shanghai International Circuit — a place that has never rewarded guesswork. Red Bull’s call to pit Jason Roy under the opening safety car was bold in theory but handed the whole strategic initiative to Ferrari. Roy had pole. He had the lead. Then Red Bull saw Väisänen on hard tyres and decided to copy the offset, sacrificing track position for a compound match. The front‑right wheel gun stuck, yes, but the error was the decision itself. They handed the race to Maranello on a plate, and Roy spent the rest of the afternoon charging through traffic for a podium that felt more like a funeral. Quarter Kevu, meanwhile, managed his VCARB machinery with the calm intelligence of a defending champion, claiming second without ever looking flustered. He didn’t overreach. He didn’t panic. That’s why he’s still the most dangerous man in the field.

The circuit’s long, high‑deg second sector proved punishing for any team running on the wrong compound at the wrong moment. Mercedes, after a bright Melbourne, fell apart: Vandesteene retired on lap one, Zwart called his car a shopping trolley in a debrief that has since leaked to half the paddock, and the Silver Arrows left China with a combined two points. The data from Shanghai’s extended hard‑tyre stints and frantic pit‑stop timing windows will be studied intensively in the coming days. As the freight departs for Suzuka, several teams are understood to be bringing targeted updates, hoping to close the gap to a Ferrari machine that has now proven it can win in the wet and the dry, from the front and from eighth.

Japan’s high‑speed load profile is expected to reward aerodynamic efficiency and mechanical balance more honestly than Australia or China. That will either expose Ferrari’s weaknesses or confirm what many of us already suspect: that this season is heading in one direction, and it’s red. The question of whether anyone can close the gap is the one everyone is working towards answering. I’ve been waiting for a genuine challenge since Melbourne. I’m still waiting.

On to the next one, then.
